segments.text: Live in Salford for the very first time in this election, it's Sunak versus Starmer, the ITV debate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: It's the first head-to-head debate in the 2024 general election with these two men who want to run the country, Rishi Sunak and Keir Starmer.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, good evening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Tonight's debate is set against the toughest of backdrops.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Since the UK last went to the polls, we've had a pandemic, Putin's invasion of Ukraine, and a cost-of-living crisis which is still hitting families hard.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Huge geopolitical threats loom large, not least, of course, climate change.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We all know how tough it's been.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We don't need the politicians to remind us.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So what are these two men going to offer us?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Who are they?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And why should they earn our vote?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Can they level with us when we know that finances are almost impossibly tight?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Later tonight, you'll be able to see what other parties are offering when they're interviewed live on ITV1 by Anushka Astana.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And the conversation will continue on the national and regional news.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But now, let's get on with tonight's debate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Keir Starmer, your opening statement, please.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: This election is all about a choice.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: More of the chaos and division we've seen for the last 14 years or turning the page and rebuilding with Labour.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I have ambition for our country and have a practical plan to deliver it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I've changed the Labour Party and put it back in the service of working people.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Now I'm seeking your backing to change our country, to make it work once again for you and your family.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Rishi Sunak.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Uncertain times call for a clear plan and bold action.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So I'll keep this simple.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: In five weeks, either Keir Starmer or I will be Prime Minister.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Beyond raising your taxes and raiding your pensions, no-one knows what Labour would actually do.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But you know what I would do?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I'll cut your taxes, protect your pension, and reduce immigration.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I have a clear plan for a more secure future for you and your family.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you, Rishi Sunak.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, we invited viewers from right across the country to submit questions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We have independently selected the questioners here with us this evening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I'll also raise some points made by people who couldn't be here.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The two leaders don't know what they'll be asked.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Also here, people who'll listen to tonight's debate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Some have voted Conservative and Labour in the past and think they will do so on July the 4th.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And some have voted for other parties or are undecided.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: You can join the debate wherever you are by using the hashtag ICVdebate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And just before we go to our first question, a reminder that each leader will have 45 seconds to respond initially.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Now, our first question, on the cost of living, Paula from Huddersfield.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Paula.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Good evening, both.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I have worked since I was 15 years old and at the moment I hold a full-time stable job.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: My children have left home.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: In the last few years I've found it very difficult to make ends meet and the money simply doesn't go far enough.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: All I do is work to live.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: My food bill is nearly doubled.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I'm in arrears with my bills.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I spend my weekends back cooking so I don't have to turn on my oven in peak times.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: My savings are gone and I'm genuinely worried about my future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I don't think you actually understand how this is for people like me.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So what can you do about that?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Thank you very much indeed, Paula, for your question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Rishi Sunak.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, Paula, I know how much of a strain the last few years have put on your family finances, the family finances of everyone in our country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And that's why my priority has always been to do what I can to support you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That started with bold action, like the furlough scheme during the pandemic, and more recently, my clear plan to bring inflation back to normal.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And now, our economy is growing again, wages are rising, and crucially, we are able to now start cutting your taxes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: But I know you're only just starting to see the benefits of that, as you described.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So the choice at this election is with my clear plan and bold action to keep cutting your taxes to give you financial security or put all that progress at risk with higher taxes, £2,000 worth of higher taxes under the Labour Party.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you, Rishi Sunak.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Here's Dhan.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Paul, I'm really, really sorry to hear the position that you're in.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It must be really, really difficult on a daily basis.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And there'll be millions of people watching this who feel in a similar position to you that the cost of living crisis is really bearing down on them with constant worry.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I'm not going to pretend that
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Ukraine and Covid hasn't made things worse.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But this government has lost control.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Liz Truss crashed the economy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: They made it worse here than in other countries.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And it's people like you that are paying the price.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Because if a government loses control of the economy, as they have,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's working people that pay the price.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And Liz Truss's advisers are in the House of Lords.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: She's going to be a candidate at the next election.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Rishi Sunak is putting her forward.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: We've got to stop this chaos, got to stop the division, turn a page and start to rebuild under Labour.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you for those opening statements, 45 seconds each.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Just to turn to you first, Keir Starmer, the point that Rishi Sunak made is the economy is growing again, the plan is working, is his view.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, he says the plan is working.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: He says the plan is working.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So the question for him is, why has he called the election now?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Because if he thinks things are going to get better towards the second half of this year, why has he called it now?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: He's called it now because he knows, and I'll ask him this, he knows inflation is going to go back up.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: He knows energy prices are going to go back up in the autumn.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: That's what he's not telling you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So he says the plan is working, but I don't think he believes the plan is working, because if he thought the plan is working, he wouldn't have called this election right now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Rishi Sunak?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I think it's slightly ironic, because from the moment I got this job, Keir Starmer was telling me, call an election, call an election.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And now that we've called an election, he's saying, oh, no, don't have an election, because I don't want to tell you what I'm going to do.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And this is the choice.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: for everybody.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The plan is working, of course, I know.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Everyone is only just starting to feel the benefits of it, but inflation is back to normal, wages are growing, taxes are now being cut.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Keir Starmer would put all that progress at risk.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: He would put up everyone's taxes by £2,000.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: £2,000 in higher taxes for every working family in our country after all the hard work and sacrifice we've been through.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: That's not the right course of action.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I don't know why you want to put up people's taxes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Paul, I just don't know how you feel when you hear a Prime Minister say, having heard what you're going through, that the plan is working, it's all all right.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I meet so many people who are struggling with their bills and the Prime Minister just keeps saying, he lives in a different world, that it's all all right.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: If people are struggling with their bills, why do you want to make it harder for them by saddling them with £2,000 worth of higher taxes?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: How is that going to make Paula or anyone's life easier?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Could you respond directly to Rishi Sunak?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: This is the Prime Minister who's been part of a government that has put up tax 26 times.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: The last manifesto that they had in 2019 said, we won't put up national insurance.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: This man then put up national insurance.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: He's the British expert on tax rises.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: They're at the highest level for 70 years.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And he's got the gall to say other people are going to... And are we going to hear a lot about the past?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: in this election but this election is about the future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: This election is about the future and I'm clear that I'm going to keep cutting people's taxes as we now are.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You want to put everyone's taxes up by £2,000 and this is really important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Independent Treasury officials have costed Labour's policies and they amount to a £2,000 tax rise.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Mark my words, Labour will raise your taxes, it's in their DNA, your work, your car, your pension, you name it, Labour will tax it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I'm just going to just draw breath there a moment please gentlemen, we're going to be talking about tax again later on.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I just want to bring this part of the debate back very firmly to Paula's question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: She made it very clear how hard her life is at the moment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: You did acknowledge it, both of you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: She spends her weekends batch cooking so she doesn't have to turn her oven on during peak times.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: There is worry here, right before our eyes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: that were being echoed across the country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: One of the key points that Paula says is, she says, I don't think you understand how hard it is for people like me.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: What are you going to do about it?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: How do you actually connect with Paula?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Rishi Sunak.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, I'd ask everyone to judge me by my actions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: You saw what I did during the pandemic.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: When disaster struck our country, I implemented the furlough scheme because I knew the devastating impact it would have on people.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And you know what?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Every week when I'm out and about, someone comes up to me and tells me how furlough saved their family, saved their home, because that's who I am.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: And I don't want to put that progress at risk.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I want to keep delivering financial security by cutting people's taxes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Whereas the Labour Party want to keep putting people's taxes up and that's not going to help anyone overcome the challenges they're facing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Paula wants to know what you're going to do about it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I think what you put to the Prime Minister is how does he connect?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Paula, what I would say is this.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: My dad worked in a factory.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: He was a toolmaker.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: My mum was a nurse.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: We didn't have a lot of money when I was growing up.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And we were in a position on occasion where we couldn't pay our bills.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So I know how that feels.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In our particular case, we had our phone cut off because we didn't have the money.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So I do know the anguish of worrying when the postman comes with a bill, what is that bill going to be?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Can I pay for it?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Are you saying that Rishi...
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I think the Prime Minister quite understands the position that you and other people are in.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I do want to, if I may, just answer... No, let Richie say... No, please let Richie respond to that, please.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because this election is about the future and making people's lives better.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Now, I've got a clear plan to ease those anxieties that Paula and everyone is facing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Because things are tough, I want to make them easier by continuing to cut your taxes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: The difference of this election is Keir Starmer is going to put your taxes up by £2,000.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So if people are worried about their electricity bill, their food bill, taking their kids on a holiday, £2,000 worth of tax rises are not going to help with any of those things.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: I'll ask again, why do you want to put everyone's taxes up?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01
